title: Understanding Coprophagia: Why Dogs Eat Their Own Feces & What to Do
meta_desc: Learn why dogs eat feces (coprophagia), when to seek veterinary care (Urgency: Low), and how photos help diagnose the issue. Helpful, expert advice.
content_html:
TL;DR: While eating feces (coprophagia) is a common behavior in dogs and usually not an emergency, you should consult a vet if it is accompanied by illness. Taking a photo of the stool can help your veterinarian diagnose potential nutritional deficiencies or underlying medical conditions.

What is coprophagia and why is my dog eating poop?

Coprophagia is the technical term for the habit of eating feces. While this behavior is understandably distressing for pet parents, it is a relatively common occurrence in dogs that can be caused by:

  • Puppyhood exploration or maternal instincts to keep a den clean
  • Behavioral triggers or anxiety
  • Nutritional deficiencies
  • Underlying medical conditions that affect digestion

Is it an emergency if my dog eats feces?

The urgency level for coprophagia is categorized as Low, as it is rarely an immediate medical crisis. However, you should seek urgent veterinary care if the behavior is accompanied by acute symptoms such as:

  • Frequent vomiting
  • Severe diarrhea
  • Lethargy
  • Sudden weight loss

How can taking a photo of my dog's stool help with veterinary triage?

While it may feel awkward, capturing a clear photo of your dog's stool provides vital clues for your veterinary team by allowing them to assess:

  • The stool's consistency and color
  • The presence of visible parasites or undigested food
  • Whether your dog is properly absorbing nutrients
  • The necessity of a diagnostic fecal test or digestive supplements

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Coprophagy, the ingestion of feces, is a relatively common behavioral problem in dogs. While the exact cause is often multifactorial and not fully understood, potential contributing factors include dietary deficiencies, malabsorption, underlying medical conditions such as exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, intestinal dysbiosis, and behavioral issues like attention-seeking or anxiety. Diagnosis requires a thorough physical examination to rule out underlying medical conditions and fecal examination to check for parasites or maldigestion. Treatment strategies involve addressing any identified underlying medical problems, dietary modification with increased fiber and digestive enzymes, behavioral modification techniques, and ensuring adequate environmental enrichment and mental stimulation.

Chapter: General

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2027)

faqs.question: Can my dog get parasites from eating poop?
faqs.answer: Yes. While eating their own 'clean' waste is less risky, consuming the feces of other animals can expose your dog to intestinal parasites like roundworms, hookworms, or protozoa like Giardia.

faqs.question: Could my dog be hungry or missing nutrients?
faqs.answer: Sometimes. Coprophagia can be linked to a diet that is lacking specific enzymes or nutrients, or conditions like Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency (EPI) that prevent the dog from feeling full.

faqs.question: How can I stop this behavior at home?
faqs.answer: The most effective method is immediate cleanup of the yard. You can also work on 'leave it' commands and consult your vet about food additives designed to make the stool taste unappealing to the dog.
